chunks.text: S. Shankar Sastry is a Professor and Director at UC Berkeley's EECS department. Contact information includes office at 333B Cory Hall, phone 510-642-1857, and email sastry@eecs.berkeley.edu. Research assistant Annie Ren can be reached at 353 Cory Hall, annie@erso.berkeley.edu.

Shankar Sastry earned his B.Tech from IIT Bombay in 1977, followed by M.S. in EECS (1979), M.A. in Mathematics (1980), and Ph.D. in EECS (1981) from UC Berkeley. He holds faculty appointments in Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences, Bioengineering, and Mechanical Engineering. He served as Dean of the College of Engineering from 2007 to 2018 and was Director of CITRIS and the Banatao Institute. Previously, he chaired the EECS department from 2001 to 2004 and was Director of DARPA's Information Technology Office in 2000. He also directed Berkeley's Electronics Research Laboratory from 1996 to 1999. He is the NEC Distinguished Professor and joined UC Berkeley's faculty in 1983 after being a professor at MIT.

Sastry's research areas include Artificial Intelligence (AI), Control, Intelligent Systems, and Robotics (CIR), Cyber-Physical Systems and Design Automation (CPSDA), Security (SEC), and Signal Processing (SP). He is associated with multiple research centers such as Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for New Media (BCNM), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), Center for Information Technology Research in the Interest of Society - The Banatao Institute (CITRIS), Center for the Theoretical Foundations of Learning, Inference, Information, Intelligence, Mathematics and Microeconomics at Berkeley (CLIMB), CITRIS People and Robots (CPAR), FHL Vive Center for Enhanced Reality, Foundations Of Resilient CybEr-physical Systems (FORCES), Partners for Advanced Transit and Highways (PATH), Robotics and Intelligent Machines Laboratory, Technology Competitiveness and Industrial Policy Center (TCIP), and Verified Human Interfaces, Control, and Learning for Semi-Autonomous Systems (VeHICaL).

Sastry teaches courses including EECS C106B: Robotic Manipulation and Interaction (TuTh 09:30-10:59, Soda 306), EECS C206B: Robotic Manipulation and Interaction (TuTh 09:30-10:59, Soda 306), and EE 290-4: Learning Enabled Multi-Agent Systems (TuTh 14:00-15:29, Cory 521).

Selected publications include works on pricing in dynamic games (2012), flow control over wireless networks (2011), robust face recognition via sparse representation (2009), reachability analysis of stochastic hybrid systems (2007), control over lossy networks (2007), multi-agent tracking with sensor networks (2007), hybrid geometric reduction (2006), flapping flight for robotic insects (2006), distributed reputation systems (2006), generalized principal component analysis (2005), assisted living technology (2005), 3D vision (2004), Markov chain Monte Carlo for tracking (2004), Kalman filtering with intermittent observations (2004), quantum operations (2004), surgical simulation software (2004), cyber defense technology (2004), and affine hybrid systems (2004).

Honors and awards include the Rufus Oldenburger Medal (2021), Berkeley Citation (2018), International Federation for Automatic Control (IFAC) Fellow (2017), Chang Lin Tien Leadership in Education Award (2010), John R. Ragazzini Education Award (2005), American Academy of Arts and Sciences Member (2003), National Academy of Engineering (NAE) Member (2001), Institute of Electrical & Electronics Engineers (IEEE) Fellow (1995), Donald P. Eckman Award (1990), and NSF Presidential Young Investigator (PYI) (1985).
